ontology.foodex2_description: Salami — cured and fermented sausage product from mixed red meats

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of salami is approximately 3 slices (about 30g), commonly used as a cold cut on sandwiches, charcuterie boards, or as a pizza topping.

nutrient_source.title: Salami, beef and pork — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Salami, beef and pork (CR ID: F06110019). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and select micronutrients not reported in HPB record; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Salami, beef and pork (FDC ID: 174608) to supplement fatty acid profile and additional micronutrients.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous low-carbohydrate cured meat products; University of Sydney GI Database consulted
gi_source.source_match_notes: Salami, beef and pork contains only 2.4g carbohydrate per 100g. No direct GI value for salami is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 28 based on analogous low-carbohydrate processed meat products (e.g., frankfurter/sausage analogues) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently fall in the low GI range (GI 20–35) due to negligible available carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per 30g serving = (28 × 2.4 × 0.30) / 100 = 0.7, which is negligible. This food elicits a minimal glycemic response in practice.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1740mg per 100g — nearly three times the 600mg/100g threshold for high-sodium foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid salami entirely; a single 30g serving delivers approximately 522mg sodium, representing over one-third of the 1500mg daily sodium target recommended by the American Heart Association for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Ultra-processed red meat with high saturated fat (~10.5g/100g) and estimated trans fat (~0.5g/100g), independently associated with increased Type 2 Diabetes risk per ADA guidelines
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption of beef and pork salami; as a processed red meat it is independently associated with increased T2D incidence, and its high saturated and trans fat content further impairs insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (10.5g/100g), trans fat (~0.5g/100g), cholesterol (89mg/100g), and sodium (1740mg/100g) collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should strictly limit or eliminate beef and pork salami from their diet, as its combination of saturated fat, trans fat, high cholesterol, and extreme sodium content significantly raises LDL cholesterol and arterial blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory profile from arachidonic acid (dual red meat source), trans fat, saturated fat, and sodium nitrates used in curing
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid beef and pork salami; the combination of arachidonic acid from two red meat sources, trans fat, high saturated fat, and sodium nitrates from curing agents creates a compounded pro-inflammatory burden that can worsen joint inflammation and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salami, beef and pork is a cured and fermented sausage product made from a blend of ground beef and pork, seasoned with salt, spices, and curing agents including sodium nitrate. The mixture is stuffed into casings and undergoes a controlled fermentation and air-drying process that concentrates flavour and reduces moisture, resulting in a firm, dense, sliceable texture with a characteristic tangy, savoury, and slightly spicy taste. Originating from Italian charcuterie traditions, beef and pork salami is widely consumed globally as a cold cut on sandwiches, charcuterie boards, pizza toppings, and antipasto platters. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 336 kcal per 100g, with high fat (25.9g), significant protein (21.85g), and very low carbohydrate (2.4g). Its sodium content of 1740mg per 100g is exceptionally high, making it one of the most sodium-dense processed meats available. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 10.5g per 100g and trans fat at approximately 0.5g per 100g. Clinically, this food is strongly contraindicated for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its extreme sodium load, high saturated and trans fat content, and its classification as an ultra-processed red meat independently associated with increased cardiometabolic risk.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in salami?
Is beef and pork salami high in fat?
Is salami bad for high blood pressure?
Can I eat salami if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F06110019) with confidence 0.95. Fatty acid sub-fractions (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for beef and pork salami. All primary values are from approved sources.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0g per 100g: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — salami is a meat-based product containing no plant fibre. Saturated fat (10.5g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (12.1g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.5g/100g) are cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID: 174608) for beef and pork salami, as HPB record does not report fatty acid sub-fractions. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo record for this specific salami entry; follow-up enrichment from USDA FoodData Central is recommended. Glycemic index is estimated at 28 (low GI) based on analogous low-carbohydrate processed meat products from the University of Sydney GI Database; carbohydrate content is only 2.4g/100g and the food elicits a negligible glycemic response in practice. GL per 30g serving is 0.7. Halal status is not confirmed as pork is a primary ingredient; is_halal is set to false. Gluten-free status reflects typical formulation; consumers with coeliac disease should verify individual product labels for cross-contamination risk.

Updated gi_category from 'not_applicable' to 'low'. Updated glycemic_load_per_serving from 0.0 to 0.7. Updated gi_source to reflect analogous_estimate basis with full documentation. Updated notes to reflect GI estimation approach.
